_note: Raven Data Insights — Calls Database. For 2025+: weight=2 (important), weight=1 (normal), weight=0 (passive/barely significant). For 2022-2024: no weight field; outcome='success' or 'failed'. Pending calls use outcome='pending'. _placeholder=true marks entries not yet filled.

calls.title: France: Debt Spiral & Political Paralysis
calls.description: France's worsening debt-to-GDP ratio, combined with political paralysis following snap elections, would push the Fifth Republic toward a constitutional and financial credibility crisis. Bond spreads versus Germany would widen materially.

calls.title: European Populist Wave Reshapes Continent
calls.description: Right-wing populist parties would gain power or expand influence across Germany, Hungary, Austria, Netherlands, and the UK as traditional centrist parties collapse amid economic anxiety, migration pressures, and loss of institutional trust.

calls.title: US Markets Outperform Europe on AI Boom
calls.description: US equity markets would significantly outperform their European counterparts throughout 2025, driven by the AI investment boom, stronger economic fundamentals, and corporate earnings resilience.

calls.title: Gaza: No Ceasefire — Siege Continues
calls.description: The Gaza siege would persist throughout 2025 with catastrophic displacement and casualty figures reaching 90%+ of the population. No meaningful or durable ceasefire would materialize despite international pressure.

calls.title: China Maintains Pressure Campaign on Taiwan
calls.description: Beijing would maintain an aggressive pressure campaign against Taiwan — military exercises, economic coercion, and diplomatic isolation — without escalating to direct armed conflict.

calls.title: Syria: Assad Regime Collapses
calls.description: The Assad regime's structural vulnerabilities — economic collapse, Russian forces depleted in Ukraine, exhausted military — would culminate in a complete regime collapse. Predicted before the fall became consensus.

calls.title: Ukraine Ceasefire Largely Complete by End 2025
calls.description: A negotiated ceasefire would largely be in place by end of 2025. This call did not materialise — talks had barely begun and peace remained a very long way off by year-end.

calls.title: Oil Prices Remain Structurally Capped
calls.description: Oil prices remain suppressed due to structural oversupply and weak global demand. Russia requires $65–70/barrel to balance its budget but sells at ~$62/barrel — a slow fiscal bleed that constrains its war economy.

calls.title: Gold Stays Elevated Above Record Highs
calls.description: Gold prices remain elevated above 2025 record highs throughout 2026, sustained by persistent geopolitical uncertainty, central bank buying programs, and real-rate dynamics.

calls.title: Ukraine Settles Into Frozen Conflict
calls.description: The Ukraine war settles into a frozen state — too costly to escalate to decisive victory, too politically risky to formally abandon for either side. No meaningful peace deal or substantial territorial change emerges in 2026.

calls.title: No Chinese Military Invasion of Taiwan
calls.description: China does not launch a military invasion of Taiwan in 2026. Pressure without war remains Beijing's preferred posture — an invasion carries too great an economic cost and military risk given current PLA readiness assessments.

calls.title: China Advances Toward EUV Chip Independence
calls.description: China makes measurable progress toward domestic EUV lithography capability within a 5–10 year horizon, gradually reducing the strategic leverage that Taiwan and ASML hold over Beijing's semiconductor ambitions.

calls.title: EU Remains Institutionally Intact
calls.description: The EU does not implode despite historic right-wing electoral gains across France, Netherlands, Germany, and Poland. Economic hardship in the automotive and chemical sectors continues, but the institutional framework holds.

calls.title: Japan-China Tensions Intensify
calls.description: Japan experiences increased friction with China over Taiwan, the Senkaku Islands, and historical grievances. PM Takaichi's government fails to resolve Japan's structural demographic or monopoly market problems.

calls.title: US AI Bubble Does Not Burst in 2026
calls.description: Despite inflated valuations and weak fundamental justifications for many AI companies, the bubble does not fully deflate in 2026. Momentum, capital inflows, and narrative power carry it further than fundamentals would suggest.

calls.title: US Labor Market Deteriorates Materially
calls.description: US labor market weakens materially in 2026 — rising credit delinquencies, unemployment creeping upward, 42M+ on food assistance. Private credit markets show first serious stress before public equity markets register cracks.

calls.title: Japan: Takaichi's Fiscal Gamble Risks a Yen Currency Crisis
calls.description: PM Takaichi's unfunded investment spree would not cause a bond market crisis — the BoJ owns too much of the JGB market — but would instead trigger a full yen currency crisis. An MoF/BoJ FX intervention would spook global equity markets by forcing an unwind of the endemic yen carry trade, with serious collateral damage for PE funds invested in Japan.
calls.article_title: Carry On? Japan's Election Could Unwind the Trade

calls.title: EU Sidelined from Ukraine Peace Negotiations
calls.description: The EU would have zero seat at the Ukraine peace table, because it had provided only a fraction of US military and financial support while simultaneously buying Russian gas. Peace talks would happen without European participation — confirmed when the US and Russia met in Riyadh, with Europe explicitly excluded.

calls.title: France Bond Spreads Widen vs Germany in 2025
calls.description: French government bond spreads versus Germany would widen further in 2025 — already at their widest since the GFC when this was written. Le Pen's political campaigns to topple the government, combined with chronic fiscal irresponsibility, would intensify the pressure. New elections would only add uncertainty.

calls.title: Trump Tariffs Trigger Global Trade Escalation
calls.description: Trump's return to the White House would bring sweeping tariffs against China and potentially the EU, triggering retaliatory responses and a broader restructuring of global trade flows along political rather than economic lines.

calls.title: Germany Chooses Stagnation Over European Leadership
calls.description: Germany — theoretically capable of being Europe's growth engine — would choose the status quo and thus irrelevance, refusing to stimulate its economy or provide credible continental leadership amid a collapsing government coalition.

calls.title: Ukraine: No Ceasefire in Q2/Q3 2025
calls.description: A negotiated settlement would land in Q2/Q3 2025 after another pointless summer offensive. Putin couldn't sustain the economic and military costs of fighting on multiple fronts. This call was wrong — despite Riyadh talks beginning, no formal ceasefire was in place by end of 2025.

calls.title: No Taiwan Invasion in 2025 — PLA Not Ready
calls.description: Xi would not annex Taiwan in 2025. Despite massive domestic economic pressures — historic youth unemployment, demographic collapse, real estate crisis — the PLA was not sufficiently ready, and there was nothing to gain militarily at that stage. The pressure campaign would continue but stop short of invasion.

calls.title: Belgium Gets Poorer Regardless of June 2024 Election
calls.description: Every Belgian party's election program, as calculated by the Federal Planning Bureau, would widen the budget deficit by 4–8% by 2029 relative to the Maastricht norm. Like Liz Truss before them, Belgian politicians looked at the mini-budget disaster and treated it as a shining example rather than a warning. The country would become significantly poorer no matter who won.
calls.article_title: Election Time: Sharks, Blood and Budgets

calls.title: Nvidia: The AI Hardware Call Pays Off
calls.description: Made the Nvidia buy recommendation to clients when it first attracted renewed attention after the crypto mining boom subsided — and was laughed out of a meeting for it. The thesis: Nvidia's GPU architecture was uniquely positioned to power the coming AI revolution. By 2024, the call had been spectacularly validated, with Nvidia becoming one of the most valuable companies on earth.

calls.title: Healthcare & Financials: Safest Returns of 2024
calls.description: Despite Magnificent Seven dominance of headline returns, the safest fundamental bets of 2024 were healthcare and financials: averaging 9–10% price returns with minimal downside volatility. Proof that buy-and-hold fundamental investing still works even in years dominated by three-trillion-dollar gorillas.

calls.title: Gaza Conflict Derails Saudi-Israel Normalization — Iran & Russia Win
calls.description: The Hamas attack and Israel's disproportionate response would completely derail the Saudi-Israel normalization process that had been the biggest strategic prize of recent Middle East diplomacy. The primary beneficiaries: Iran and Russia, who needed the deal to fail. The conflict would also entrench far-right Israeli governance and make any two-state resolution structurally impossible.
calls.article_title: Welcome to the New Dark Ages

calls.title: Ukraine-Russia War Settles Into Prolonged Stalemate
calls.description: Russia lacked the bureaucratic depth and economic capacity to reconstitute empire. The invasion would not produce a decisive Russian victory, nor a Ukrainian one — it would grind into a costly stalemate where money and weapons from the West kept Ukraine afloat while Russia bled out its military capacity.
calls.article_title: Welcome to the New Dark Ages

calls.title: Iran Approaching Nuclear Weapons Threshold
calls.description: Iran was approaching the status of a de facto tenth nuclear power. With nine nations already possessing nuclear weapons across five factions, Tehran's enrichment program was on a trajectory that would eventually put it in the same category — with enormous destabilising consequences for the Middle East.
calls.article_title: Welcome to the New Dark Ages

calls.title: China's Demographic Peak: Structural Decline Now Locked In
calls.description: China's population had already peaked and structural long-run decline was now locked in — the country faced losing roughly half its population over the next 30–40 years. Unlike EU demographic problems that could theoretically be addressed with managed immigration, China's demographic trajectory was essentially irreversible.

calls.title: 2024: The Most Consequential Election Year in Modern History
calls.description: The 2024 global election cycle — with votes in the US, EU, India, UK, and numerous other major democracies — would be the single most consequential electoral period in modern history. Voters choosing between reformers and the anti-establishment wave would set the direction of the world for a decade.
calls.article_title: Welcome to the New Dark Ages

calls.title: Liz Truss Mini-Budget Will Destroy Her Government
calls.description: Truss and Kwarteng's £45 billion unfunded tax-cut mini-budget was described as madness — inviting inflation to new record highs, risking irreparable economic damage, and ignoring every warning from the IMF, senior Tories, and the Bank of England. Sterling would fall further, the BoE would be forced into emergency intervention, and the political consequences would be terminal. Truss resigned 21 days later.

calls.title: Peak Inflation Imminent; Rate Hiking Cycle Near Its End
calls.description: Peak inflation was imminent in Q3 2022, with two or three additional rate hikes likely before the Fed and ECB cycle ended. Markets had priced in a brief but substantial recession. For cash holders, deploying into markets would beat losing to inflation. Both the peak inflation call and the rate cycle endpoint proved broadly correct.

calls.title: Germany's Russia Energy Dependency Is a Strategic Trap
calls.description: Germany was paying Russia €200 million per day for gas and oil even while trying to support Ukraine — not by choice, but because Russian contracts were simply cheaper than any available alternative. This structural energy dependency was a strategic trap that would take years and enormous cost to escape, and would constrain Germany's foreign policy throughout the conflict.

calls.title: Russia Cannot Win the Ukraine War
calls.description: Russia had failed every stated war goal, created a global food-supply shock, couldn't depose the president of a corrupt second-tier state, and was fighting the last war with its grandparents' equipment. Putin was desperately seeking a face-saving exit. Russia could not come out on top — its best outcome was a negotiated settlement before complete military and economic exhaustion.

calls.title: Belgium: Government Will Fall in 2023
calls.description: The De Croo coalition — a haphazard assembly of seven parties scraping by on a thin majority — would probably collapse at some point in 2023, forced by the EU to finally curb its runaway spending. A wrong call: the government limped on until the scheduled June 2024 elections, propped up more by the absence of a viable alternative than by any coherent governing purpose.

calls.title: European Nuclear Renaissance: Energy Crisis Reverses Policy
calls.description: Europe's energy crisis would force a reversal of the decades-long anti-nuclear consensus. The brutal reality of needing affordable baseload energy — combined with restarting coal plants as the alternative — would force governments to begin public tenders for Small Modular Reactors and life-extensions for existing nuclear capacity.

calls.title: Interest Rates Won't Surge Much Higher Through 2023
calls.description: Given the mountains of debt requiring annual refinancing, it would be unreasonable for rates to climb substantially from late-2022 levels by end of 2023. Both the Fed and ECB would slow and ultimately pause their hiking cycles well before rates reached truly damaging territory for government balance sheets.

calls.title: Russia Ends Up With 2–3 Provinces in South-East Ukraine at Best
calls.description: Written to clients at the very start of Russia's invasion: Russia's best possible outcome would be ending up with 2–3 provinces in the South-East of Ukraine, not the conquest or regime change they sought. Confirmed three years later when Russia held Donetsk, Luhansk, Zaporizhzhia and parts of Kherson — precisely the South-Eastern fringe — while negotiations began from a position of exhaustion on both sides.
